project.name: localocr_extended
project.description: Privacy-first self-hosted household operations platform: turns grocery/restaurant/bill receipts into living inventory, shopping, budgeting & analytics via a multi-model OCR pipeline (Gemini->OpenAI->Ollama->Anthropic). Flask/Python backend + vanilla-JS SPA, plus a Flutter/Dart Android client.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what LocalOCR Extended is: a privacy-first, self-hosted household operations platform that turns receipts into living inventory, shopping lists, budgets, and analytics. It lays out the core story the rest of this tour follows — capture a receipt, run multi-model OCR, update domain state, and drive the next shopping trip. The README also maps the 39 backend modules, 15 Flask blueprints, 25 DB tables, and the 4-provider OCR fallback chain you'll meet shortly.

tour.title: Flask Application Factory
tour.description: The backend is the heart of the system, and everything boots from this application factory. It builds the Flask app, wires secrets and the auth decorators (require_auth / require_write_access), registers all 15 blueprints, manages the DB session lifecycle, and bootstraps the admin user. With a fan-in of 21, it is the second-most depended-upon module in the codebase — reading it gives you a bird's-eye view of the runtime structure.

tour.title: Data Model & Schema Bootstrap
tour.description: This module is the single most depended-upon file in the project (fan-in 45): it defines the SQLAlchemy ORM models for all 25 tables, the engine and session factories, runtime column migrations, and default AI-model seeding. Every domain in Steps 5–9 reads and writes through these models, so understanding the data vocabulary here unlocks the rest of the backend. The initial table layout (users, receipts, products, stores) is created by the first Alembic migration shown later in Step 11.
tour.languageLesson: SQLAlchemy 2.0 declarative models map Python classes to database tables. The session factory pattern centralizes connection and transaction management, and runtime column migrations let the app reconcile schema drift on startup before Alembic migrations run.

tour.title: The Multi-Model OCR Pipeline
tour.description: This is the signature feature of LocalOCR. Building on the data model from Step 3, the extraction pipeline runs OCR with fallback, classifies and validates the receipt, then persists purchases, bill metadata, and inventory updates. The routing layer sits beneath it, encrypting provider API keys with Fernet, resolving the active model selection, tracking usage and cost, and dispatching inference to the chosen provider. Together they implement the README's capture → clean-line-items step.

tour.title: Vision Provider Adapters
tour.description: These four adapters form the Gemini → OpenAI → Ollama → Anthropic fallback chain that the router from Step 4 dispatches into. Each exposes the same receipt-extraction contract, so the pipeline can transparently fall back when a provider fails or is unavailable. The Gemini adapter is the richest (enrichment, PDF text fallback, image compression, daily usage tracking), while the Ollama adapter adds local-model health and pull helpers for fully offline operation — the privacy-first promise.

tour.title: Receipt Upload & Inventory Truth
tour.description: Once OCR produces clean line items, they flow into inventory. The upload module (the largest backend file) handles receipt upload, retrieval, editing, dedup/merge, Plaid linking, and attribution. The write-path helpers upsert inventory from receipt items and apply manual truth patches, while the active-inventory module recomputes the canonical 'truth' of what the household currently has from purchases plus manual adjustments. This is the README's inventory-updates step.

tour.title: Shopping & Recommendations
tour.description: With inventory truth established, the platform closes the loop by driving the next shopping trip. The recommendation engine generates, filters, and groups shopping suggestions and detects price deals, seasonal patterns, and low-inventory items. The shopping-list blueprint then manages sessions, items, share links, recommendation confirmations, photo identification, and finalization — turning inventory gaps into an actionable list, exactly as the README narrates.

tour.title: Budgeting, Bills & Analytics
tour.description: This cluster covers the spend-tracking half of the platform across the grocery, dining, expenses, and bills domains. The budgeting-domains module normalizes spending domains and budget categories (fan-in 7), the household-budget module sets monthly budgets and computes rollups, bill projections combine recurring entries with actual bills and cash transactions, and the analytics module produces the spending, per-person, store-comparison, and recurring-obligation summaries that feed the dashboard.

tour.title: Authentication & Chat Assistant
tour.description: Two cross-cutting backend capabilities round out the server. The authentication module (fan-in 5) handles password/session auth, trusted-device pairing, QR login, access links, service accounts, and Google OAuth — the gate every blueprint sits behind via the decorators from Step 2. The chat assistant builds a household-data context from the spending, shopping, and inventory data of Steps 6–8 and dispatches completions across the same four-provider fallback chain you saw for vision in Step 5.

tour.title: The Web SPA Shell
tour.description: Now we move from server to client. The vanilla-JS single-page application lives in one HTML shell that contains the full app markup, inline scripts, and every page view, all consuming the backend blueprints from Steps 6–9. Its look is driven by a centralized design-token source — a machine-readable JSON defining colors, typography, spacing, radius, and motion for the Apple-inspired design system — which is also the source of truth for the mobile client's theme.

tour.title: Flutter Android Client
tour.description: The project also ships a Flutter/Dart Android client that ports the SPA. The entry point bootstraps a Riverpod ProviderScope and initializes config before rendering the root LocalOcrApp widget, which wires go_router and theme into a MaterialApp.router. The root provider graph supplies secure storage, the cookie jar, and the Dio-based ApiClient (fan-in 4) that talks to the same Flask API — so the client is a thin presentation layer over the backend you just toured.
tour.languageLesson: Flutter uses Riverpod for dependency injection: a ProviderScope at the root makes providers (storage, API client, auth) available throughout the widget tree, while go_router declaratively maps routes to screens with auth-redirect guards.

tour.title: Database Migrations
tour.description: The schema you met in Step 3 evolves through Alembic migrations. The initial migration creates the core tables (users, receipts, products, stores), and 33 numbered revisions layer on features — Plaid tables, shared dining, the medicine cabinet, true-inventory status, and Telegram session tracking. Reading the migration sequence is the fastest way to see how the data model grew alongside the product.
tour.languageLesson: Alembic migrations are ordered, reversible revisions with upgrade() and downgrade() functions. Each revision references its predecessor (down_revision), forming a linear history the app can replay to bring any database to the current schema.

tour.title: Containerization & Deployment
tour.description: Finally, see how everything ships. The Dockerfile builds a python:3.11-slim image, installs poppler-utils (for PDF receipts) and the requirements, copies the app, migrations, scripts and design assets, exposes port 8090, and launches via an entrypoint that auto-applies the migrations from Step 12. docker-compose orchestrates the Flask backend alongside optional Mosquitto MQTT and Ollama services across bridge networks with named volumes — a complete self-hosted stack, while the Makefile drives the design-token asset pipeline that feeds both clients.
tour.languageLesson: docker-compose declares multi-service stacks: each service gets its own image, named volumes persist data across restarts, and bridge networks isolate internal traffic. Here the optional MQTT and Ollama services let the stack run fully offline for privacy.
